  "opinion": "As a 45-year-old electrician living in Naples with a part-time job, I’ve seen how social media discussions about vaccines can feel overwhelming, especially when you’re already struggling with depression. My parents had different experiences with health—my father never got vaccinated as a kid, and my mother was skeptical about medical interventions. That’s why I’ve always been cautious about health information, but now, with the constant stream of misinformation online, it’s hard to know what to believe. I’ve tried to stay informed by checking official health sites, but the emotional toll of seeing people argue about vaccines—especially on platforms where I spend hours daily—is exhausting. My high neuroticism makes me prone to anxiety, so I’ve learned to set boundaries: I limit my time on social media after work, focus on my running and yoga to clear my mind, and talk to my wife about what I’m seeing. When disagreements arise, I avoid getting drawn into arguments and instead share facts from trusted sources. It’s not perfect, but it helps me manage the stress. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend several hours daily on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a 45-year-old electrician living in Naples with a part-time job, I’ve seen how social media discussions about vaccines can feel overwhelming, especially when you’re already struggling with depression. My parents had different experiences with health—my father never got vaccinated as a kid, and my mother was skeptical about medical interventions. That’s why I’ve always been cautious about health information, but now, with the constant stream of misinformation online, it’s hard to know what to believe. I’ve tried to stay informed by checking official health sites, but the emotional toll of seeing people argue about vaccines—especially on platforms where I spend hours daily—is exhausting. My high neuroticism makes me prone to anxiety, so I’ve learned to set boundaries: I limit my time on social media after work, focus on my running and yoga to clear my mind, and talk to my wife about what I’m seeing. When disagreements arise, I avoid getting drawn into arguments and instead share facts from trusted sources. It’s not perfect, but it helps me manage the stress. The key is to prioritize mental health over the need to be right, especially when you’re already feeling depressed. If you’re overwhelmed, step back, breathe, and remember that your well-being matters more than the noise online.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 45-year-old depressed man with high neuroticism who spends hours on social media. His background in a lower education context and family history of health skepticism shapes his cautious approach to vaccines. The opinion focuses on practical coping strategies aligned with his hobbies (running/yoga) and mental health needs. It avoids technical jargon, emphasizes emotional regulation, and ties to his real-world context (Naples, electrician work). The tone is empathetic yet pragmatic, acknowledging his depression without minimizing it.
citations: World Health Organization (WHO) guidelines on vaccine misinformation
